Subscriber's View of Xbox Live

The primary motivations for players to subscribe to Xbox Live are to access content not available offline and to interact (either competitively or cooperatively) with other players online. Of the two of these, interaction with other players is by far more compelling for and important to the subscriber. The ability to play games with remote subscribers is a feature whose impact should not be underestimated. It is literally true that a significant number of subscribers eagerly await the release of Xbox Live titles that enable them to play cooperative games far-distant friends and relatives.

This section of the Xbox Live documentation discusses these two motivations in the following topics.

  • Accessing Extra Content
  • Online Communities

Accessing Extra Content

To help your customers feel that they are getting more for their money, Xbox Live enables players to download free content that you provide. For more information, see Downloading Content from Xbox Live. Your title can download content even without requiring players to subscribe to Xbox Live. However, if they do subscribe, you can enable them to use Xbox Live to purchase new levels, game items, expansion packs, and so forth. This ability not only enhances the initial appeal of your game, it can vastly extend the game's lifetime.

In addition, depending on the type of game you produce, it is possible for you to offer a "Best of Xbox Live" collection. Such a collection usually consists of levels, game items, and so forth that players create with tools shipped with your game. You enable players to share such items with you and with each other by using the Xbox Live storage feature. For more information, see Storage

Online Communities

The result of subscribers' eagerness for online interaction is that subscribers often view Xbox Live as a place for forming communities around game titles. Making the effort to build the community infrastructure for your titles can have a significant positive effect on both online and offline revenues. Online, subscribers are more likely to pay for premium content if they feel they are part of the game's community. Offline, subscribers are more apt to recommend your title to others if they have a stronger emotional connection to it. Online communities help form and strengthen that emotional connection.

Players online want to be able to connect easily with their friends and relatives. To make this easier and more intuitive, your title uses the Xbox Live friends and presence features. These features provides players with a list of subscribers they consider their friends. At any time, players can add or remove other players from the list. The list enables players to find out if their friends are online and, if so, what games the friends are playing. They can then send messages to invite friends to play games together.

When friends play together, they find the experience much more compelling if they can talk to each other while the game is in progress. Your title adds this functionality by using the voice chat feature.

One way to build communities is by enabling players to form teams or clans in which they can play against other teams or clans. The Xbox Live teams feature performs much of the work of providing teams in the online version of your title. The formation of teams enables you to provide formal and informal competitions. Note that competitions do not require teams, but teams require competitions.

You can enhance the intensity of the competition experience by keeping statistics on all participants. The Xbox Live statistics and game clips features provide the functionality your title requires to make this happen. Games that use the statistics and game clips features give their players "bragging rights" about particularly good performances. This helps form a cadre of skilled players that become the core of your game's community.

If your title is not conducive to the formation of teams or competitions, you can still provide players with the opportunity to play together. Use the matchmaking feature to help players find game sessions to join.

Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
